revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

revision.comment: new article
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kibitsu jinja'''|吉備津神社|}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsu-jinja-bingo/ "Kibitsu Jinja (Bingo)"]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== ''Kitbitsuhiko jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bingo Province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]|吉備津彦命||}},<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of [[Emperor Korei]].<ref>Louis-Frédéric, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{nihongo|'''Kibitsu jinja'''|吉備津神社|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko jinja'''|吉備津彦神社|}}, is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsu-jinja-bingo/ "Kibitsu Jinja (Bingo)"]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== ''Kitbitsu jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bingo Province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]|吉備津彦命||}},<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of legendary [[Emperor Korei]].<ref>Louis-Frédéric, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref>
The shrine was one of 50 of the lowest ranked, nationally significant shrines ({{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社|}}.
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kibitsuhiko jinja]] * [[Kibitsu jinja (Bitchu)]]

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kibitsu jinja'''|吉備津神社|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Kibitsuhiko jinja'''|吉備津彦神社|}}, is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsu-jinja-bingo/ "Kibitsu Jinja (Bingo)"]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== ''Kitbitsu jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bingo Province]]. <ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]|吉備津彦命||}},<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of legendary [[Emperor Korei]].<ref>Louis-Frédéric, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref>
The shrine was one of 50 of the lowest ranked, nationally significant shrines ({{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社|}}.
The ''[[Honden]]'' dates from 1648.<ref>[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]], Database of National Cultural Properties, [http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=3193 ID# 3193]; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kibitsuhiko jinja]] * [[Kibitsu jinja (Bitchu)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Uncategorized|date=November 2012}}

revision.text:
{{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu jinja'''|吉備津神社|}}, also known as '''Kibitsuhiko jinja''', is a Japanese [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] in the Chūgoku region of the island of [[Honshu]].<ref name="kotodamayama">Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kibitsu-jinja-bingo/ "Kibitsu Jinja (Bingo)"]; excerpt, "Kibitsuhiko Jinja ( 吉備津神社 ) Bingo province’s most important shrine ....; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref>
==History== ''Kitbitsu jinja'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') of [[Bingo Province]].<ref>[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-11-20.</ref> The enshrined ''[[kami]]'' is {{nihongo|[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]|吉備津彦命||}},<ref name="kotodamayama"/> who was the son of legendary [[Emperor Korei]].<ref>Louis-Frédéric, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA513&dq= "Kibitsu-hiko no Mikoto"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 513.</ref>
The shrine was one of 50 of the lowest ranked, nationally significant shrines ({{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社|}}).
The ''[[Honden]]'' dates from 1648.<ref>[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]], Database of National Cultural Properties, [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193 ID# 3193] <!-- dead link ... see http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kibitsu_Jinja&diff=428595769&oldid=428204256 ...? --></ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

{{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shinichicho, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shinichicho neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[Emperor Kōrei]], conquerer of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[Nanboku-cho period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

revision.text:
{{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shinichicho, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shinichicho neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[Nanboku-cho period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]]

吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shinichicho, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shinichicho neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]] [[Category:Kokuhei Kosha]]

revision.text:
{{Other places|Kibitsu jinja}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shinichicho, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shinichicho neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[Nanboku-cho period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]]

吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shinichicho, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shinichicho neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[Nanboku-cho period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]]

revision.comment: Disambiguating links to [[Kibitsu Shrine]] (link changed to [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)]]) using [[User:Qwertyytrewqqwerty/DisamAssist|DisamAssist]].
revision.text:
{{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[Nanboku-cho period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]]

The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]] [[Category:Kibi clan]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[Nanboku-cho period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]] [[Category:Kibi clan]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]] [[Category:Kibi clan]] [[Category:Dd]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|34|9.61|N|133|16|15.85|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = [[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[Nanboku-cho period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Komainu]]''', [[Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:806 establishments]] [[Category:Kibi clan]] [[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]]
