revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|qid=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | prefecture = {{ill|Fukuyama|qid=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|qid=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|qid=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|qid=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|qid=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|qid=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|qid=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|qid=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)]] 吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6404042]] Wikidata name: Kibitsu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Fukuyama, Hiroshima|Fukuyama]] in [[:en:Hiroshima Prefecture|Hiroshima Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Bingo Province|Bingo Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]], conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, [[:en:Emperor Kōrei|Emperor Kōrei]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[:en:Kō no Moroyasu|Kō no Moroyasu]] issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the shrine was supported by [[:en:Mōri Terumoto|Mōri Terumoto]], and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the [[:en:Fukushima Masanori|Fukushima clan]] and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuyama Domain|Fukuyama Domain]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from [[:en:Shin-ichi Station|Shin-ichi Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuen Line|Fukuen Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by [[:en:Mizuno Katsushige|Mizuno Katsushige]]. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]''', [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360
