revision.comment: imported only latest revision from https://archive.org/details/miraheze-wiki-shintowiki-20251218 to avoid excessive disk usage, comment on the revision was Text replacement - "Shikinai Shosha" to "Shikinai Shōsha"
revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|WD=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}}, conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|WD=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|WD=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|WD=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|WD=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|WD=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|WD=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|WD=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}}, conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|WD=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|WD=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|WD=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|WD=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|WD=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|WD=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|WD=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}}, conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|WD=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|WD=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|WD=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|WD=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|WD=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|WD=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125|1=Shin'ichi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|WD=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province|1=Bingo Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}}, conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|WD=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu|1=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|WD=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto|1=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan|1=Fukushima Masanori}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|WD=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain|1=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|WD=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station|1=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|WD=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line|1=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|WD=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige|1=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125|1=Shin'ichi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|WD=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province|1=Bingo Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}}, conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|WD=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu|1=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|WD=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto|1=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan|1=Fukushima Masanori}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|WD=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain|1=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|WD=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station|1=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|WD=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line|1=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|WD=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige|1=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|WD=Q661038|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|WD=Q11502125|1=Shin'ichi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|WD=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|WD=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province|1=Bingo Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}}, conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|WD=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|WD=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu|1=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|WD=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto|1=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|WD=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan|1=Fukushima Masanori}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|WD=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain|1=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|WD=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station|1=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|WD=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line|1=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|WD=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige|1=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|WD=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (福山市)]] {{wikidata link|Q6404042}} [[Category:806 establishments]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu revision.text:
[[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[co:commons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)]][[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]][[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]][[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuyama, Japan}} {{Other places|Kibitsu Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kibitsu Shrine
吉備津神社 | image = File:Kibitsu shurine01.JPG | caption = Honden of Kibitsu Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hiroshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.56922222|N|133.27102778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kibitsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Kibitsu | deity = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|qid=Q65318|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 400 Miyauchi, Shin'ichi-chō, Fukuyama-shi, Hiroshima-ken 729-3104 | website = {{Official|1=https://bingokibitujinja.com}} | architecture_style = | festival = November 23 | leadership = | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}}} | location = {{ill|Fukuyama|qid=Q661038|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}}; {{ill|Shin'ichi|qid=Q11502125|1=Shin'ichi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Kibitsu Shrine'''|吉備津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Shin'ichi-chō neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|qid=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} in {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Bingo Province|en|Bingo Province|qid=Q1049113|lt=Bingo Province|1=Bingo Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on November 23.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kibitsu Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto]]'''| 吉備津彦命|}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|qid=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}}, conqueror of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]] * {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Nekohikofuto-no-mikoto'''| 大日本根子彦太瓊命|}}, {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|qid=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Kuwashi-hime]]'''| 細比売命 |}}, empress of Emperor Kōrei. * {{nihongo|'''Wakatake-hiko-no-mikoto'''| 稚武彦命|}}, younger brother of Kibitsuhiko ==History== The origins of Kibitsu Jinja are uncertain. The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|qid=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}. However, there is no documentary evidence to support this, and the shrine does not appear in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', which was complied between 905 and 967 AD. The first time the shrine is mentioned in a historical source is in 1148 in which the name is mentioned in the records of [[Yasaka Shrine]], and [[archaeological excavation]]s on the grounds have found not artifacts earlier than the 12th century. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|qid=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu|1=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|qid=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|qid=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto|1=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|qid=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan|1=Fukushima Masanori}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|qid=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain|1=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|qid=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station|1=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|qid=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line|1=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|qid=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige|1=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|qid=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine02.JPG|Kagura stage（Hiroshima Prefecture ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 上随神門.JPG|Upper Gate File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 下随神門.JPG|Lower Gate（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu shurine03.JPG|Great Toriii（Fukuyama city ICP） File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 公孫樹.JPG|Gingko Tree File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 御池.JPG|Pond ==See also== *[[Ichinomiya]] *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|qid=Q9644839|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hiroshima)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Kibitsu-jinja (Fukuyama)}} *[http://bingokibitujinja.com Official home page] {{Kibi clan}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bingo)]] [[arz:كيبيتسو جينجا]] [[ja:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] [[zh:吉備津神社 (福山市)]] {{wikidata link|Q6404042}} [[Category:806 establishments]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Bingo Province]] [[Category:Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] The shrine claims that when {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|qid=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} was divided into three provinces in 806, it was established as a ''[[bunrei]]'' from the original [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}. However, from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, it was regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province, and had a large number of estates, with which it often clashed with secular authorities. During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the shrine is the location where Imperial loyalist Sakurayama Koretoshi (桜山茲俊) raised an army in 1331 in support of the [[Southern Court]]. However, as detailed in the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', after receiving a false report that [[Kusunoki Masashige]] had been defeated at [[:en:Akasaka Castle|Akasaka Castle]], he committed suicide with his wife and children and burned the shrine down.[https://web.archive.org/web/20050312054126/http://www.city.fukuyama.hiroshima.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shisekibu/30.html Fukushima City home page] This event led to the area around the shrine to be designated as a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1934.{{cite web |url= https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/139457 |title=一宮（桜山慈俊挙兵伝説地）|trans-title=Ichinomiya (Sakurayama jishun kyohei densetsu-chi)|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} {{ill|Kō no Moroyasu|en|Kō no Moroyasu|qid=Q11669872|lt=Kō no Moroyasu|1=Kō no Moroyasu}} issued a decree in 1346 ordering the Bingo ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|qid=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' to stop harassing the shrine. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was supported by {{ill|Mōri Terumoto|en|Mōri Terumoto|qid=Q1142657|lt=Mōri Terumoto|1=Mōri Terumoto}}, and in the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]] it was supported by the {{ill|Fukushima Masanori|en|Fukushima Masanori|qid=Q1194995|lt=Fukushima clan|1=Fukushima Masanori}} and the [[Mizuno clan]] who were ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|Fukuyama Domain|qid=Q909278|lt=Fukuyama Domain|1=Fukuyama Domain}}. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was listed as a {{nihongo|National Shrine, 3rd Rank|国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} in 1871.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located a twenty-minute walk from {{ill|Shin-ichi Station|en|Shin-ichi Station|qid=Q7497149|lt=Shin-ichi Station|1=Shin-ichi Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuen Line|en|Fukuen Line|qid=Q899394|lt=Fukuen Line|1=Fukuen Line}}.{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Properties=== *'''[[Honden]]''', constructed in 1648 as a donation by {{ill|Mizuno Katsushige|en|Mizuno Katsushige|qid=Q8190277|lt=Mizuno Katsushige|1=Mizuno Katsushige}}. It is a seven by four bay hall in the relatively large and has a flat "Yomazukuri" style which is common in the Bingo and Aki regions {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/3193|title=吉備津神社本殿|trans-title=Kibitsu Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|qid=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}}''', {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, group of three with height of 78, 80 and 82 centimeters. As Komainu come in sets of two, one is missing. The statues are now kept at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/4465|title=木造狛犬|trans-title=Mokuzo Komainu|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *'''[[Tachi]]''', {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. Set of four. In 1972, the sword fittings were stolen and their whereabouts are unknown. The blades are currently deposited at the Okayama Prefectural Museum.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6358|title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（二字不明）次郎左エ門尉忠吉／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6357 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘備州尾道五阿弥長行天文廿四年六月吉日／吉備津宮奉寄進御太刀（以下不明）／拵付〉|trans-title=Sumiyoshi-sha Horaku Hyakushu Waka Strip|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6359 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6360 |title=毛抜形太刀〈銘正光／拵付〉|trans-title=|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:吉備津神社 (福山市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden（Fukuyama city ICP） File:Kibitsu
